justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1, BR-4, BR-6 as invariant: true; BR-2, BR-3, BR-5 as invariant: false). Each rule has a detailed written justification that clearly distinguishes state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. Rules 2, 3, and 5 explicitly use 'configurable' reasoning. The grace period ambiguity is surfaced in both the escalations section and confidence/low_confidence_areas. The recommendation to store policies as configuration is implicit in the invariant_justification fields (e.g., 'a product leader could reasonably change this') and in the testable assertions using parameterized values (e.g., 'N (currently 3)'). Output goes well beyond minimum requirements with acceptance criteria, edge cases, business impact analysis, and additional ambiguities surfaced.

observations.text: All 6 invariant classifications are correct with no errors
Justifications are exceptionally detailed, clearly articulating why each rule is or is not a state integrity constraint
Grace period ambiguity surfaced explicitly in escalations with three options and a recommendation
Configurable parameters use parameterized notation in testable assertions (e.g., 'N (currently 3)'), implicitly recommending configuration over hard-coding
Additional valuable edge cases identified beyond the rubric requirements: processor outages vs declines, tier upgrade mid-retry, voluntary vs auto cancellation
Sequential vs concurrent trial distinction correctly identified as an edge case
No explicit standalone recommendation to 'store policies as configuration' — the idea is woven into justifications but not called out as a top-level architectural recommendation
